children.name: Cettia
children.description: Cetti's warbler and its Asian relatives — skulking, rounded passerines of dense undergrowth. Cetti's Warbler is renowned for its explosive, loud song that seems impossible for such a small, secretive bird. The genus is named after Italian zoologist Francesco Cetti.

children.name: Cettia cetti
children.description: A stocky, chestnut-brown warbler with a rounded tail and a pale supercilium. Despite its skulking nature, its song is one of the loudest and most startling of any European bird — a sudden burst of ringing notes that erupts from dense waterside vegetation. Expanding northwards in Europe; increasingly recorded in southern Sweden. Resident year-round.

children.name: Cettia brunnea
children.description: A small bush warbler found from the Himalayas through southern China and Southeast Asia. Warm brown above, pale below with brown flanks. Skulks in thick undergrowth, often near water. Its song is a series of clear, descending whistles.

children.name: Cisticola
children.description: Cisticolas — a large genus of small, streaky warblers found across Africa, Asia, and southern Europe. They are named for their characteristic 'cisticola' buzzing or trilling songs, often given in aerial displays above grassland. Over 50 species, many of which are notoriously difficult to identify in the field.

children.name: Cisticola juncidis
children.description: A tiny, streaky brown bird of wet grasslands and rice paddies. Its name comes from its song — a monotonous, repeated 'zit-zit-zit' given in a distinctive bouncing display flight. One of the most widespread Old World passerines. Occurs around the Mediterranean and is expanding northwards.

children.name: Cisticola erlangeri
children.description: Endemic to the Ethiopian highlands. Similar to the Zitting Cisticola but darker and more richly coloured. Inhabits montane grassland and moorland above 2,000 metres.

children.name: Cisticola albiscutata
children.description: Found in eastern and southern Africa. Distinctive rufous wing panels and a heavily streaked back. Inhabits moist grassland and marshes. Its display flight is accompanied by a dry, insect-like buzzing.

children.name: Prinia
children.description: Prinias — long-tailed, active warblers of Africa and Asia, often called 'long-tailed warblers'. They hold their slender tails cocked at an angle and flick them characteristically. About 25 species, many of which have adapted well to human-modified habitats like farmland and gardens.

children.name: Prinia gracilis
children.description: A delicate, pale prinia with a very long, graduated tail. Buff-brown above, whitish below. Found across the Middle East, South Asia, and northeastern Africa. Inhabits dry scrub, tamarisk thickets, and reedbeds. Its tail is constantly in motion, wagging and fanning.

children.name: Prinia socialis
children.description: A common resident of the Indian subcontinent. Slaty-grey above in breeding plumage, warmer brown in winter. Chestnut wing panel. Inhabits scrub, grassland, and gardens. One of the few prinias with distinct breeding and non-breeding plumages.

children.name: Prinia subflava
children.description: The most widespread African prinia. Warm brown upperparts, buffy-white underparts, and a long cocked tail. Found across sub-Saharan Africa in grassland, scrub, and cultivation. Its song is a rapid, repetitive trill.
